title: The Most Efficient Machine Ever Built
summary: A B2 paragraph passage arguing that the humble bicycle, not any high-powered engine, is the most efficient machine humans have made, and why that fact unsettles our idea of progress.
body:
In a small repair workshop on a side street in Nairobi, a mechanic spends his mornings doing something that looks almost too modest to matter: he wipes grit from bicycle chains and drips a little oil onto each link. The work is unglamorous, and the machine he tends is more than two centuries old in its essential design. Yet by one measure that engineers take very seriously, that machine is the most efficient ever built. A well-maintained bicycle converts roughly ninety per cent of the energy a rider puts into the pedals directly into forward motion. No car, no aircraft, no power station comes close to that figure. What makes the claim so striking is not that the bicycle is fast, for it plainly is not, but that almost nothing is lost between effort and result.

The reason lies in the drivetrain, the unfashionable chain of parts that links the pedal to the wheel. Most machines waste energy as heat, as noise, as friction in places no one ever sees. A petrol engine throws away the majority of the fuel it burns before the car has moved a centimetre. The bicycle chain, by contrast, runs over hardened teeth with so little rubbing that the losses are tiny, and what little is lost can be reduced still further by the very task the Nairobi mechanic performs each morning. It is the oil, not some breakthrough in materials, that keeps the figure high. The lesson is awkward for an age that prizes invention: the gains here come not from building something new but from caring for something old.

It would be a mistake, however, to read this as mere nostalgia. The point is not that older is always better; plenty of old machines were wasteful and have rightly been abandoned. What the bicycle reveals is subtler. We tend to assume that progress means adding power, that a more advanced machine is one that does more, faster, with a larger engine behind it. The bicycle quietly refutes that assumption. It does less, and in doing less it loses almost nothing, and it is precisely because it loses almost nothing that it can be driven by a body that produces only a fraction of the power of a small motor. Efficiency, here, is not a consolation prize for the weak. It is the whole achievement.

There is a further, uncomfortable implication. Had the twentieth century treated efficiency rather than power as the mark of a good machine, the cities built around it might look very different. Instead, the measure that won out was speed, and speed has an appetite for energy that no amount of clever engineering can fully satisfy. The cyclist, who carries his own engine and feeds it with breakfast, was treated for decades as a relic, something to be tolerated at the edge of the road. That judgement is now being quietly reversed in places as different as Copenhagen, Bogotá, and Nairobi itself, not out of sentiment but out of arithmetic. When the true cost of moving a person from one place to another is added up, the machine that wastes the least begins to look less like a relic and more like the future. The mechanic with his can of oil, it turns out, has been maintaining not a museum piece but a quiet argument against the idea that more must always mean better.

annotations.meaningVi: vứt bỏ phần lớn nhiên liệu

annotations.phrase: treated for decades as a relic
annotations.meaningVi: bị coi như đồ cổ lỗi thời suốt nhiều thập kỷ
vì lý do tính toán (số học), không vì cảm tính
một hiện vật bảo tàng

grammarNotes.title: Cleft sentence with 'It is ... that'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu chẻ nhấn mạnh: 'It is the oil, not some breakthrough in materials, that keeps the figure high.' Cấu trúc 'It is + phần nhấn mạnh + that + phần còn lại' làm nổi bật yếu tố quan trọng nhất.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The 'It is ... that' cleft highlights one element: 'It is the oil, not some breakthrough in materials, that keeps the figure high.' This focuses attention on the true cause.

grammarNotes.title: Emphatic cause: 'it is precisely because ... that'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Cấu trúc nhấn mạnh nguyên nhân: 'it is precisely because it loses almost nothing that it can be driven by a body...'. Dùng để khẳng định mạnh mẽ mối quan hệ nhân quả.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: An emphatic causal cleft: 'it is precisely because it loses almost nothing that it can be driven by a body...'. It strongly foregrounds the reason behind a result.

grammarNotes.title: Inverted third conditional
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu điều kiện loại 3 đảo ngữ nói về giả định trái với quá khứ: 'Had the twentieth century treated efficiency rather than power as the mark of a good machine, the cities... might look very different.' Tương đương 'If the twentieth century had treated...'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: An inverted third conditional speculates about an unreal past: 'Had the twentieth century treated efficiency... as the mark of a good machine, the cities might look very different' (= If the twentieth century had treated...).

grammarNotes.title: Concession with 'It would be a mistake to read this as...'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Cách bác bỏ một cách hiểu sai: 'It would be a mistake, however, to read this as mere nostalgia.' Tác giả thừa nhận một phản bác có thể có rồi loại bỏ nó, một kỹ thuật nghị luận tinh vi.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: A concessive move that pre-empts a misreading: 'It would be a mistake, however, to read this as mere nostalgia.' The writer raises a likely objection and dismisses it.

translationVi:
Trong một xưởng sửa chữa nhỏ trên một con phố nhánh ở Nairobi, một người thợ máy dành những buổi sáng của mình để làm một việc trông gần như quá khiêm tốn để đáng kể: anh lau bụi bẩn khỏi các sợi xích xe đạp và nhỏ một chút dầu lên từng mắt xích. Công việc ấy chẳng hào nhoáng, và cỗ máy mà anh chăm sóc đã hơn hai thế kỷ tuổi trong thiết kế cốt lõi của nó. Vậy mà, theo một thước đo mà các kỹ sư rất coi trọng, cỗ máy đó là cỗ máy hiệu quả nhất từng được chế tạo. Một chiếc xe đạp được bảo dưỡng tốt chuyển hóa khoảng chín mươi phần trăm năng lượng mà người đạp bỏ vào bàn đạp trực tiếp thành chuyển động về phía trước. Không một chiếc ô tô, máy bay hay nhà máy điện nào đạt gần tới con số đó. Điều khiến lời khẳng định này gây ấn tượng không phải vì xe đạp nhanh, vì rõ ràng nó không nhanh, mà vì gần như chẳng có gì bị mất đi giữa nỗ lực và kết quả.

Lý do nằm ở hệ truyền động, chuỗi bộ phận không thời thượng nối bàn đạp với bánh xe. Hầu hết các cỗ máy lãng phí năng lượng dưới dạng nhiệt, dưới dạng tiếng ồn, dưới dạng ma sát ở những nơi không ai nhìn thấy. Một động cơ xăng vứt bỏ phần lớn nhiên liệu nó đốt trước khi chiếc xe nhúc nhích được một xăng-ti-mét. Sợi xích xe đạp, trái lại, chạy trên những răng đã tôi cứng với rất ít cọ xát đến mức tổn thất là rất nhỏ, và phần ít ỏi bị mất đó còn có thể được giảm thêm nữa nhờ chính công việc mà người thợ ở Nairobi thực hiện mỗi sáng. Chính dầu, chứ không phải một bước đột phá nào về vật liệu, mới là thứ giữ cho con số ấy cao. Bài học này thật khó nghe đối với một thời đại đề cao phát minh: lợi ích ở đây đến không phải từ việc chế tạo một thứ gì đó mới mà từ việc chăm sóc một thứ cũ kỹ.

Tuy nhiên, sẽ là sai lầm nếu hiểu điều này chỉ là sự hoài niệm thuần túy. Vấn đề không phải là cũ thì luôn tốt hơn; có vô số cỗ máy cũ vốn lãng phí và đã bị loại bỏ một cách đúng đắn. Cái mà chiếc xe đạp phơi bày ra tinh tế hơn thế. Chúng ta có xu hướng giả định rằng tiến bộ nghĩa là thêm sức mạnh, rằng một cỗ máy tân tiến hơn là cỗ máy làm được nhiều hơn, nhanh hơn, với một động cơ lớn hơn đứng sau nó. Chiếc xe đạp lặng lẽ bác bỏ giả định đó. Nó làm ít hơn, và chính vì làm ít hơn nên nó gần như chẳng mất gì, và chính vì gần như chẳng mất gì nên nó có thể được vận hành bởi một cơ thể chỉ tạo ra một phần nhỏ sức mạnh của một động cơ nhỏ. Hiệu quả, ở đây, không phải là một giải an ủi dành cho kẻ yếu. Nó chính là toàn bộ thành tựu.

Còn có một hàm ý sâu xa và khó chịu hơn nữa. Giả sử thế kỷ hai mươi đã xem hiệu quả chứ không phải sức mạnh là dấu hiệu của một cỗ máy tốt, thì những thành phố được xây dựng quanh nó có lẽ đã trông rất khác. Thay vào đó, thước đo giành chiến thắng lại là tốc độ, và tốc độ có một sự thèm khát năng lượng mà không một kỹ thuật khéo léo nào có thể thỏa mãn hoàn toàn. Người đi xe đạp, người mang theo động cơ của chính mình và nuôi nó bằng bữa sáng, suốt nhiều thập kỷ bị coi như đồ cổ lỗi thời, một thứ gì đó chỉ được dung thứ ở rìa đường. Phán xét ấy nay đang được lặng lẽ đảo ngược ở những nơi khác nhau như Copenhagen, Bogotá, và chính Nairobi, không phải vì cảm tính mà vì lý do tính toán. Khi chi phí thật sự của việc đưa một người từ nơi này đến nơi khác được cộng lại, cỗ máy lãng phí ít nhất bắt đầu trông ít giống một đồ cổ hơn và giống tương lai hơn. Người thợ máy với chiếc bình dầu của anh, hóa ra, đã và đang bảo dưỡng không phải một hiện vật bảo tàng mà là một lập luận lặng lẽ chống lại cái ý niệm rằng nhiều hơn thì luôn phải nghĩa là tốt hơn.

questions.prompt: What is the Nairobi mechanic doing in the opening paragraph?
options: Cleaning bicycle chains and oiling each link
questions.explanation: The text says 'he wipes grit from bicycle chains and drips a little oil onto each link.'
questions.hint: Read the first sentence describing his morning work.

questions.prompt: By what measure is the bicycle called the most efficient machine ever built?
options: It converts about ninety per cent of the rider's energy into forward motion
questions.explanation: The passage states a well-maintained bicycle 'converts roughly ninety per cent of the energy a rider puts into the pedals directly into forward motion.'
questions.hint: Look for the percentage figure in the first paragraph.

questions.prompt: Why is the claim about the bicycle 'so striking', according to the writer?
options: Because the bicycle is extremely fast
Because it is made of rare materials
Because almost nothing is lost between effort and result
questions.explanation: The text says it is striking 'not that the bicycle is fast... but that almost nothing is lost between effort and result.'
questions.hint: See the final sentence of the first paragraph, which corrects a likely guess.

questions.prompt: Where does the bicycle's efficiency mainly come from?
questions.explanation: The passage says 'The reason lies in the drivetrain, the unfashionable chain of parts that links the pedal to the wheel.'
questions.hint: The second paragraph opens by naming the key part.

questions.prompt: How do most machines, unlike the bicycle, lose energy?
questions.explanation: The text says 'Most machines waste energy as heat, as noise, as friction in places no one ever sees.'
questions.hint: Look at the second paragraph's list of where energy is wasted.

questions.prompt: What keeps the bicycle's efficiency figure high, according to the writer?
options: The oil applied by the mechanic
questions.explanation: The passage states 'It is the oil, not some breakthrough in materials, that keeps the figure high.'
questions.hint: Find the cleft sentence ('It is ... that') in the second paragraph.

questions.prompt: Why does the writer call the lesson 'awkward for an age that prizes invention'?
options: Because the gains come from caring for something old, not building something new
questions.explanation: The text says 'the gains here come not from building something new but from caring for something old.'
questions.hint: Read the end of the second paragraph about new versus old.

questions.prompt: What does the writer say it would be 'a mistake' to read the passage as?
questions.explanation: The text says 'It would be a mistake, however, to read this as mere nostalgia.'
questions.hint: The third paragraph opens by warning against one interpretation.

questions.prompt: Which statement does the writer NOT claim?
options: The bicycle loses almost no energy
Older machines are always better than new ones
Efficiency is the bicycle's whole achievement
Some old machines were wasteful and rightly abandoned
questions.explanation: The writer explicitly denies this: 'The point is not that older is always better; plenty of old machines were wasteful and have rightly been abandoned.'
questions.hint: Look for where the writer corrects a possible misreading in paragraph three.

questions.prompt: What common assumption about progress does the bicycle 'quietly refute'?
options: That progress means adding power and doing more
That progress only happens in cities
questions.explanation: The text says we assume 'progress means adding power... The bicycle quietly refutes that assumption.'
questions.hint: See the third paragraph's description of what we tend to assume.

questions.prompt: What does the writer mean by saying efficiency is 'not a consolation prize for the weak'?
options: Efficiency is only for strong machines
Efficiency is the real achievement, not a lesser substitute for power
questions.explanation: The passage concludes 'Efficiency, here, is not a consolation prize for the weak. It is the whole achievement.'
questions.hint: Read the last two sentences of paragraph three together.

questions.prompt: What does the writer suggest might have happened if the twentieth century had valued efficiency over power?
options: The cities built around it might look very different
questions.explanation: The text uses an inverted conditional: 'Had the twentieth century treated efficiency rather than power... the cities built around it might look very different.'
questions.hint: Look at the opening conditional sentence of the final paragraph.

questions.prompt: According to the writer, what 'won out' as the measure of a good machine instead of efficiency?
questions.explanation: The text says 'the measure that won out was speed, and speed has an appetite for energy.'
questions.hint: See the sentence after the conditional in the final paragraph.

questions.prompt: Why is the judgement of the cyclist as a 'relic' now being reversed?
options: Out of arithmetic, when the true cost of transport is added up
Because cities ran out of fuel
questions.explanation: The passage says the reversal happens 'not out of sentiment but out of arithmetic' when the true cost of moving a person is added up.
questions.hint: Look at why Copenhagen, Bogotá, and Nairobi are changing their view.

questions.prompt: What does the final sentence suggest the mechanic has really been maintaining?
options: A quiet argument against the idea that more must always mean better
questions.explanation: The text says he has been maintaining 'not a museum piece but a quiet argument against the idea that more must always mean better.'
questions.hint: Read the closing sentence of the passage carefully.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's main argument across the passage?
options: Efficiency, not power or speed, is the true mark of a good machine
Old technology should never be replaced
questions.explanation: The whole passage uses the bicycle to argue that 'more must always mean better' is mistaken and that efficiency is the real achievement.
questions.hint: Consider the thread that connects the drivetrain, the assumption about progress, and the conclusion.

The reason lies in the {{b5}}, the unfashionable chain of parts that links the pedal to the wheel. Most machines waste energy as heat, as noise, as {{b6}} in places no one ever sees. A petrol engine throws away the {{b7}} of the fuel it burns before the car has moved a centimetre. The bicycle chain, by contrast, runs over hardened teeth with so little rubbing that the losses are {{b8}}, and what little is lost can be reduced still further by the very task the Nairobi mechanic performs each morning. It is the {{b9}}, not some breakthrough in materials, that keeps the figure high. It would be a mistake, however, to read this as mere {{b11}}. The point is not that older is always better; plenty of old machines were wasteful and have rightly been abandoned. The bicycle quietly {{b13}} that assumption. It does less, and in doing less it loses almost nothing, and it is precisely because it loses almost nothing that it can be driven by a body that produces only a fraction of the power of a small motor. Instead, the measure that won out was {{b16}}, and speed has an appetite for energy that no amount of clever engineering can fully satisfy. The cyclist, who carries his own engine and feeds it with breakfast, was treated for decades as a {{b17}}, something to be tolerated at the edge of the road. That judgement is now being quietly reversed in places as different as Copenhagen, Bogotá, and Nairobi itself, not out of sentiment but out of {{b18}}. When the true cost of moving a person from one place to another is added up, the machine that wastes the least begins to look less like a relic and more like the future. The mechanic with his can of oil, it turns out, has been maintaining not a museum piece but a quiet argument against the idea that more must always mean better.

blanks.explanation: The work looks 'almost too modest to matter.'
It is 'the most efficient ever built.'
Energy is converted 'directly into forward motion.'
The claim is striking 'not that the bicycle is fast, for it plainly is not.'
Machines waste energy 'as heat, as noise, as friction.'
A petrol engine throws away 'the majority of the fuel it burns.'
With so little rubbing 'the losses are tiny.'
'It is the oil... that keeps the figure high.'
Gains come 'from caring for something old.'
It would be a mistake to read this as 'mere nostalgia.'
Efficiency is 'not a consolation prize for the weak.'
The conditional contrasts 'efficiency rather than power.'
'The measure that won out was speed.'
The cyclist 'was treated for decades as a relic.'
The reversal happens 'not out of sentiment but out of arithmetic.'

criticalThinkingQuestion: The writer claims that the twentieth century chose speed over efficiency, and that this choice shaped our cities. If your own city had been designed around the machine that 'wastes the least' rather than the fastest one, what would change, and what might be lost? Defend your view.
